g.data-narration: Welcome to Special String Operations in Java. Learn about string pool, concatenation, and performance optimization.
Special String Operations are unique manipulation techniques that leverage Java's string immutability and memory optimization features.
g.text: Unique string manipulation techniques in Java that leverage
string immutability and JVM memory optimization features.

g.data-narration: These operations are crucial for memory-efficient development, performance optimization, and proper handling of immutable strings.
The + operator combines strings, converting non-string objects using their toString() method automatically.
The concat() method specifically joins strings but requires String arguments, throwing NullPointerException for null values.
The + operator is more flexible (works with any objects) while concat() is faster for pure string operations but less versatile.
The String Pool is a special memory area in JVM where unique string literals are stored to optimize memory usage.
String literals go to the pool, while new String() creates objects in heap. This affects memory usage and reference comparisons.
The intern() method manually adds strings to the pool, returning the canonical representation for memory optimization.
g.text: • Moves string to pool if not already present

g.data-narration: The toString() method provides string representation of objects. All Java objects inherit it from the Object class.
Using + in loops creates many intermediate String objects, causing poor performance due to string immutability.
StringBuilder maintains a mutable buffer, avoiding intermediate object creation. Use append() for efficient string building.
This diagram shows how string pool entries are unique and shared, while heap objects are separate instances.
g.text: Memory Visualization: String Pool vs Heap

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Use equals() for value comparison, avoid + in loops, and leverage string pooling for memory efficiency.
Exam tips: Remember null handling differences, string pool behavior, and when to use each concatenation method.
g.text: • + converts null to "null" string, concat() throws NullPointerException
• String literals auto-intern, new String() creates heap objects
• Use StringBuilder for dynamic string building in loops
• toString() should be overridden for meaningful output
